elements.sent-2411-001: Gravitational anomalies are obstructions to coupling a theory with a nontrivial background metric.
elements.sent-2411-002: In the case of 2-dimensional conformal field theories (CFT), the gravitational anomaly manifests itself as the chiral central charge $c_{- =c-\bar{c}$ \cite{Kim22ccc,Fan22ccc , \ie the difference between the central charges of the chiral part and the anti-chiral part of the theory\footnote{Theories with $c_{- =0$ are said to be gravitational anomaly free or have a trivial gravitational anomaly.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-003: Besides, an alternative definition of gravitational anomaly is used in Ref.~\cite{Kong14grav , where it is defined by the absence of a lattice realization.}
elements.sent-2411-004: In this definition, our construction identifies higher central charge as a new indicator of gravitational anomalies.. .
elements.sent-2411-005: In practice, it is related to and often measured by thermal Hall conductance $\kappa=\frac{\pi {6 Tc_{-}$ \cite{Kane97thermalHall,Banerjee18thermal,Kapustin20thermal where $T$ is the temperature .}}}
elements.sent-2411-006: We will use the terms “gravitational anomaly” and “chiral central charge” interchangeably in the following.
elements.sent-2411-007: An important consequence of gravitational anomalies is that they are obstructions to an energy-conserving boundary condition of the field theory.
elements.sent-2411-008: In addition, $(d+1)$-dimensional field theories with a nontrivial gravitational anomaly cannot emerge from a $d$-dimensional lattice model\footnote{Through out this paper, we always assume lattice models are tensor-factorized with local interactions.}
elements.sent-2411-009: We also use lattice regularizations and lattice realizations interchangeably.
elements.sent-2411-010: Moreover, by the dimension of the lattice model, we mean the spatial dimension.
elements.sent-2411-011: Although these statements has been known for a long time as folklore, it has been systematically studied only recently \cite{Jensen18bdy,Thorngren21bdy,Hellerman2021grav and was proved in the 2-dimensional case in Ref.~\cite{Hellerman2021grav .}}
elements.sent-2411-012: The basic ingredients in Ref.~\cite{Hellerman2021grav will be reviewed in the next section.}
elements.sent-2411-013: Despite above progress, there are still many open problems.
elements.sent-2411-014: For example, it is conjectured in Ref.~\cite{Hellerman2021grav (see also conjecture 6.3 of Ref.~\cite{Moller2024VOA ) that the vanishing of gravitational anomaly (\ie $c_{- =0$) in 2d CFT implies an energy-conserving boundary condition \cite{Cardy2004BCFT,Frohlich2007dCFT,Recknagel_Schomerus_2013,Li2024BCFT .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-015: Furthermore, it is also natural to ask, given a field theory with vanishing gravitational anomaly, whether it is always possible to realize it as the low energy effective field theory of some lattice model.
elements.sent-2411-016: In the present paper, we answer both of these questions negatively.
elements.sent-2411-017: Our argument is based on earlier results obtained in Ref.~\cite{Hellerman2021grav as well as a newly developed notion called higher central charges \cite{Ng2018higher,Ng2020highercentralcharge,Kaidi2022highercentralcharge,Kobayashi2022boundary,kobayshi2024highercentralcharge and Witt equivalence of chiral CFTs \cite{Moller2024VOA .}}}
elements.sent-2411-018: In summary, higher central charges can serve as an obstruction to energy-conserving boundary conditions and lattice regularizations beyond gravitational anomaly.
elements.sent-2411-019: This section serves as a recap of the main results of Ref.~\cite{Hellerman2021grav .}
elements.sent-2411-021: Let us also denote their energy-momentum tensor by $T(z)$ and $\bar{T (\bar{z )$ respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-022: Assuming our theory lives on the upper half plane which has the real axis as its boundary (as in panel (a) of Fig.~\ref{fig:folding ).}
elements.sent-2411-023: On the real axis, the conformal boundary condition is imposed \beq T(z)|_{z\in\R =\bar{T (\bar{z )|_{\bar{z}\in\R \eeq The physical meaning of this boundary condition is that there is no energy flowing out of our system, \ie conformal boundary conditions are nothing but energy-conserving boundary conditions.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-024: An immediate consequence of this boundary condition is that $c_{- =c-\bar{c =0$.}}
elements.sent-2411-025: To wit, we note the $TT$-OPE \beq \begin{aligned T(z)T(w) =\frac{c {2(z-w)^{4 +\frac{2T(w) {(z-w)^{2 +...}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-026: Conversely, this means a non-vanishing chiral central charge $c_{-}$ obstructs conformal boundary conditions.
elements.sent-2411-027: To see why a nonzero $c_{-}$ implies the absence of lattice regularization, we first assume that our 2d CFT with nonzero $c_{-}$ can be regularized by a lattice model.
elements.sent-2411-028: However, for a lattice model with local interactions, it is always possible to impose the free boundary condition, \ie one just removes half of the chain.
elements.sent-2411-029: It is argued in Ref.~\cite{Friedan2006boundary , a critical spin chain with free boundary condition can be described by a 2d CFT with conformal boundary condition Eq.~\eqref{eq:conformal_BC in the infrared (IR) limit\footnote{More precisely, one has to assume that the bulk of this chain can be described by some CFT, bulk correlation function satisfies cluster decomposition in the IR and a well-defined thermodynamic limit.}}}
elements.sent-2411-031: The fermionic case is studied in Ref.~\cite{Boyle_Smith21boundaryRG .. .}
elements.sent-2411-032: A more physical way to see this is to note, conformal boundary conditions are nothing but energy-conserving boundary conditions of a CFT.
elements.sent-2411-033: At the lattice level, free boundary condition is manifestly energy-conserving, so if we assume the bulk of the chain flows to a CFT in the IR, the free boundary condition must flow to a conformal boundary condition of the IR CFT.
elements.sent-2411-035: Thus, if a 2d CFT admits a lattice regularization, then it must admit a conformal boundary condition, which corresponds to the free boundary condition in the ultraviolet (UV).
elements.sent-2411-036: Therefore it contradicts the existence of gravitational anomaly (\ie $c_{-}\not=0$).
elements.section-2411-003: From conformal boundary to topological interface
elements.sent-2411-037: In this section we will focus on 2d rational CFTs.
elements.sent-2411-038: By the famous TQFT/CFT correspondence \cite{Witten:1988hf,Cabra97TFTCFT,Witten975brane,Gawedzki99CFT, Fuchs02TFTCFT , these rational CFTs can be viewed as boundary theories of certain 3d topological quantum field theories (TQFTs).}
elements.sent-2411-039: In the following, we will present a construction relating the existence of {conformal boundary conditions of the 2d rational CFTs to the existence of {topological boundary condition of TQFTs in the 3d bulk.}}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=0.9\linewidth]{Folding_new.png} \caption{(a) A 2d CFT is defined on upper half plane $H_{+}:=\{z\in\bbC|\mathrm{Im}(z)\geqslant 0\}$ and the conformal boundary condition $T(z)|_{z\in\R}=\bar{T}(\bar{z})|_{\bar{z}\in\R}$ is imposed on the real axis $\mathrm{Im}(z)=0$. The physical meaning of this boundary condition is that no energy flows outwards. (b) By folding trick, the anti-chiral degrees of freedom (DoF) are then defined on the lower half plane $H_{-}$. (c) By conformal transformation, $H_{+}$ is mapped to the upper hemisphere where $\T$ lives on. Similarly, $\bar{\T}$ lives on the lower hemisphere and the conformal boundary condition is then imposed on the equator. (d) Re-apply the folding trick so that the topological interface $S$ becomes a topological boundary.} \label{fig:folding}
elements.sent-2411-040: The construction starts from the panel (a) of Fig.~\ref{fig:folding .}
elements.sent-2411-041: Since the chiral degrees of freedom (DoFs) and anti-chiral DoFs are decoupled, one can always use the folding trick so that the anti-chiral theory $\bar{\T}$ is moved from $H_{+}$ to $H_{-}$ (see panel (b) of Fig.~\ref{fig:folding ).}
elements.sent-2411-042: After the folding step, we use conformal transformation to compactify our system on a sphere, so that the chiral theory $\T$ lives on the upper hemisphere and $\bar{\T}$ lives on the lower hemisphere.
elements.sent-2411-043: The conformal boundary (\ie the real axis in panel (b)) is mapped to the equator of the sphere $S^{2}$.
elements.sent-2411-044: Note that the chiral theory $\T$ is not a well-defined 2d field theory itself\footnote{It is called a relative field theory in math literature, see Ref.~\cite{Freed2012relative for more details.}}
elements.sent-2411-045: Similarly, anti-chiral theory $\bar{\T}$ must be defined on the boundary of a 3d TQFT $\bar{\cC}$ (see panel (c) of Fig.~\ref{fig:folding ).}
elements.sent-2411-046: However, $\cC$ and $\bar{\cC}$ are different in general.
elements.sent-2411-047: The only way for the diagram in panel (c) to be consistent is to have a topological interface $S$ separating $\cC$ and $\bar{\cC}$.
elements.sent-2411-048: This interface $S$ must be topological because by construction, all gapless DoFs live on the sphere $S^{2}$ and all DoFs in the bulk are gapped.
elements.sent-2411-049: The conformal boundary condition is hence specified by the choice of such a topological interface $S$ and the boundary condition of $S$ (the red line in panel (c)).
elements.sent-2411-050: This generalizes the construction in Ref.~\cite{Fuchs02TFTCFT,Kapustin2010surface where $\T$ and $\bar{\T}$ are taken to be isomorphic hence it follows $\cC=\bar{\cC}$ and $S$ becomes a surface operator in theory $\cC$ rather than a topological interface between different theories.}
elements.sent-2411-051: Since the interface $S$ is topological, after re-applying the folding trick, it can be viewed as the topological boundary of the theory $\cC\times\bar{\cC}$.
elements.sent-2411-052: Note that our total theory $\T\times\bar{\T}$ can be viewed as a boundary theory of the total bulk theory $\cC\times\bar{\cC}$ and each step above is actually reversible, we have obtained the following.
elements.sent-2411-053: The 2d RCFT $\T\times\bar{\T}$ admits a conformal boundary condition only if its bulk TQFT $\cC\times\bar{\cC}$ admits a topological boundary condition.
elements.sent-2411-054: In mathematical literature, if two TQFTs (labeled by $\cC$ and $\bar{\cC}$) are separated by a topological interface, we say $\cC$ and $\bar{\cC}$ are Witt equivalent \cite{Fuchs13bicat .}
elements.sent-2411-055: Recently, the notion of Witt equivalence is generalized to strongly rational vertex operator algebras (\ie rational chiral CFTs) in Ref.~\cite{Moller2024VOA .}
elements.sent-2411-056: Two rational chiral CFTs are said to be (weakly) Witt equivalent if they have the same central charge and their representation categories are Witt equivalent as modular tensor categories.
elements.sent-2411-057: In this language, we found that for a 2d RCFT to admit conformal boundary condition, it is necessary that its chiral part is Witt equivalent to its anti-chiral part.
elements.sent-2411-058: In fact, the topological interface separating $\cC$ and $\bar{\cC}$ is uniquely determined by the conformal boundary condition.
elements.sent-2411-059: This result shares the same spirit of topological holography principle, \ie boundary physics uniquely determines the bulk.
elements.sent-2411-060: A mathematical proof to theorem \ref{thm:conf_BC and the uniqueness is available in appendix \ref{app:proof .}}
elements.sent-2411-061: As a further remark, theorem \ref{thm:conf_BC remains valid even if one considers fermionic (a.k.a spin) CFTs \cite{Hsieh2021minimal,Kulp2021minimal,Boyle_Smith2021minimal, Runkel2023spinCFT , since there is only one spin structure on disks and spheres.}}
elements.sent-2411-062: Besides, since the sphere equipped with the spin structure bounds the 3-dimensional ball, our construction applies without any modification.
elements.sent-2411-063: The problem of topological (hence gapped) boundary condition of (2+1)d topological order is completely characterized by the Lagrangian algebra anyon~\cite{Kapustin2010surface,Kapustin11boundary,Kitaev12gapped,Kong14condensation .}
elements.sent-2411-064: The lack of Lagrangian algebra hence indicates a protected gapless edge theory.
elements.sent-2411-065: However, despite being complete, it is usually complicated to find the Lagrangian algebra even if it exists.
elements.sent-2411-066: Therefore, it is desirable to find some more computable quantities to characterize the existence of topological boundary conditions.
elements.sent-2411-067: For example, if the chiral central charge $c_{-}\not=0$, then there cannot be any topological boundary condition, otherwise it would contradict the nonzero thermal Hall conductance $\kappa=\frac{\pi^{2 {3 c_{- T$, which is linear in temperature\footnote{The contribution from gapped modes is exponentially small for low $T$ \cite{Kapustin20thermal .. .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-068: The mod 8 reduction of chiral central charge is determined by the anyon data, known as the Gauss-Milgram formula \beq \xi_{1 :=\frac{\sum_{a d_{a ^{2}\theta_{a {|\sum_{a d_{a ^{2}\theta_{a | =\exp(2\pi i\frac{c_{- {8 ) \eeq where $a$ labels the type of simple anyons, $d_{a}$ is the quantum dimension of anyon $a$ and $\theta_{a}$ is the $\U$-valued topological spin of anyon $a$, see Sec.}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-069: It is interesting to ask if the chiral central charge is the only obstruction to topological boundaries and it is known for long that the answer is no \cite{Levin2013gapless, Kaidi2022highercentralcharge .}
elements.sent-2411-070: Further obstructions, known as higher central charges are proposed and studied recently.
elements.sent-2411-071: They are defined as follows \beq \xi_{n :=\frac{\sum_{a d_{a ^{2}\theta_{a ^{n {|\sum_{a d_{a ^{2}\theta_{a ^{n | \eeq It is easy to see that $\xi_{1}$ is exactly equivalent to $c_{- (\mathrm{mod}\,8)$.}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-072: It is proved in Ref.~\cite{Ng2018higher and advocated in \cite{Kaidi2022highercentralcharge that.}}
elements.sent-2411-073: Conversely, if $\xi_{n}\not=1$ for some $n$ with $\gcd(n,N_{FS )=1$, then this topological field theory cannot admit a topological boundary condition.}
elements.sent-2411-074: Combining lemma \ref{lemma:gapped with our theorem \ref{thm:conf_BC , we then deduce that.}}
elements.sent-2411-075: A 2d rational CFT with nontrivial higher central charge $\xi_{n}\not=1$ for some $n$ with $\gcd(n,N_{FS )=1$ does not admit a conformal boundary condition.}
elements.sent-2411-076: This corollary provides a further obstruction to conformal boundary condition beyond the gravitational anomaly $c_{-}$ (or $\xi_{1}$).
elements.sent-2411-077: Combining above theorem with the discussion in Ref.~\cite{Hellerman2021grav , \ie a lattice regularization implies the existence of conformal boundary condition, we then obtain that.}
elements.sent-2411-078: A 2d rational CFT with nontrivial $\xi_{n}$ for some $n$ satisfying $\gcd(n,N_{FS )=1$ cannot emerge from a lattice model with tensor-factorized Hilbert space and local interactions.}
elements.sent-2411-079: Our corollary \ref{coro:absence_lattice can be viewed as a special case of higher version of Bloch theorem \cite{Watanabe2019Bloch,Kapustin2019Bloch,Else2019criticaldrag, Watanabe2022Bloch .}}
elements.sent-2411-080: The thermal version of Bloch theorem studied in Ref.~\cite{Kapustin2019Bloch indicates that systems with nonzero thermal Hall conductivity (equivalently chiral central charge $c_{-}$) cannot emerge from a lattice model with local interactions.}
elements.sent-2411-081: Corollary \ref{coro:absence_lattice is a special case because we restrict us to the case of rational CFTs only while the original Bloch theorem applies to any quasi (1+1)d systems.}
elements.sent-2411-082: Our result is also valid for fermionic systems.
elements.sent-2411-083: For example, let us consider the abelian Chern-Simons theory $\U_{2}\times \U_{-4}$, corresponding to following $K$-matrix, \beq K=\begin{pmatrix 2 0 0 -4 \end{pmatrix \eeq all anyons are abelian hence $d_{a ^{2 =1$ for any $a$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-084: The boundary theory of this theory is described by (chiral) Luttinger liquid with following action \beq S=\frac{1 {4\pi}\int \dd^{2 x \partial_{x}\phi_{i (K_{ij}\partial_{t}\phi_{j -V_{ij}\partial_{x}\phi_{j ) \eeq where $V_{ij}$ is a non-universal matrix encoding the velocity of these chiral waves.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-085: This boundary theory contains two chiral bosons with opposite chirality, hence $c=\bar{c =1$ and $c_{- =c-\bar{c =0$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-086: Thus this model has no thermal Hall conductance.
elements.sent-2411-087: However, this model has $\xi_{3 =-1$ while $N_{FS =8$, by lemma \ref{lemma:gapped , the bulk $\U_{2}\times\U_{-4}$ Chern-Simons theory does not admit a topological boundary condition, hence the chiral Luttinger liquid described by Eq.~\eqref{eq:chiral_Luttinger cannot be gapped by local perturbations.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-088: Besides, by theorem \ref{coro:obs_con and corollary \ref{coro:absence_lattice , the chiral Luttinger liquid Eq.~\eqref{eq:chiral_Luttinger does not admit a conformal boundary condition and lattice regularization.}}}
elements.sent-2411-089: At first glance one might think the absence of lattice regularization is due to the nonzero Hall conductance.
elements.sent-2411-090: As for any charge vector $\Vec{t =(a,b)$ where $a,b\in\z$, we have electric Hall conductance (in natural units $\hbar=e=k_{B =1$) \beq \sigma_{H =\frac{1 {2\pi}\vec{t}\cdot (K^{-1}\vec{t )=\frac{1 {8\pi (2a^{2 -b^{2 )\not=0,\quad\forall\,a,b\in\z \eeq However, this is not the case.}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-091: The absence of lattice regularization is still true even if one breaks the global $\U$ symmetry explicitly, e.g. by coupling the FQHE system to an s-wave superconductor.
elements.sent-2411-092: Since an s-wave superconductor is described by a $\z_{2}$-$BF$ theory \cite{Hansson2004SC , it is in the same phase as toric code (TC).}
elements.sent-2411-093: This allows us to introduce $\U$-breaking perturbations in Eq.~\eqref{eq:chiral_Luttinger .}
elements.sent-2411-094: However, as is shown in Ref.~\cite{Ng2018higher that higher central charges are multiplicative under stacking of topological phases, thus $\U_{2}\times\U_{-4}\times\mathrm{TC}$ still has $\xi_{3 =-1$ since toric code admits a topological boundary so it has trivial higher central charges.}}
elements.sent-2411-095: Thus, we recover the results in Ref.~\cite{Levin2013gapless .}
elements.sent-2411-096: Our results indicate, even with the $\U$-breaking perturbations, the perturbed chiral Luttinger liquid still does not admit a lattice realization.
elements.sent-2411-097: Next, we discuss RCFTs enriched by global symmetries.
elements.sent-2411-098: We will focus on invertible symmetries labeled by a group $G$.
elements.sent-2411-099: Thus, besides conformal symmetry, we can require the boundary condition to preserve $G$-symmetry as well.
elements.sent-2411-100: By preserving $G$-symmetry, we mean that the boundary can absorb the $G$-symmetry defects\footnote{In Ref.\cite{Choi2023boundary , this is known as strong-symmetric boundary conditions, see Fig.~\ref{fig:absorbing .}}}
elements.sent-2411-101: The weak boundary condition means bulk symmetry defect can end on boundary topologically.
elements.sent-2411-103: It is natural to expect that the bulk TQFT admits $G$-symmetry as well.
elements.sent-2411-104: We also assume that the global symmetry on boundary symmetry extends to a global symmetry in the bulk TQFT.
elements.sent-2411-105: For finite group $G$, this is rigorously proved in Ref.~\cite{Kirillov2004Gcrossedcat , we expect it to hold for continuous symmetry as well.}
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=0.5\linewidth]{absorbing.png} \caption{For a theory defined on a disk, the (strongly) symmetric boundary $D$ can absorb symmetry defect labeled by $g\in G$.} \label{fig:absorbing}
elements.sent-2411-106: Suppose our 2d RCFT admit a $G$-symmetric conformal boundary, we again employ the folding (or doubling) trick.
elements.sent-2411-107: As before, the boundary condition $D$ gives rise to a unique topological interface inside the 3d bulk.
elements.sent-2411-108: However, we note that in the doubling procedure, one copy of the symmetry defect gets orientation-reversed (see northern hemi-sphere of Fig.~\ref{fig:doubled_absorbing ).}
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=0.5\linewidth]{doubled_absorbing.png} \caption{As is argued in previous sections, $D$ uniquely determines a topological interface in the 3d bulk (the yellow surface). The boundary symmetry defect also extends to a bulk symmetry defect (the orange surface) and can be absorbed by pushing it to the boundary.} \label{fig:doubled_absorbing}
elements.sent-2411-109: By assumption, the boundary condition $D$ remains invariant if we push the defects to the boundary (\ie the equator in Fig.~\ref{fig:doubled_absorbing ).}
elements.sent-2411-110: Since the topological interface in the bulk is uniquely determined by the boundary condition $D$, it is also invariant under the fusion with symmetry defects.
elements.sent-2411-111: After applying folding trick again, we obtain that.
elements.sent-2411-112: For a 2d RCFT with global symmetry $G$ to admit a $G$-symmetric conformal boundary conditions, it is necessary for its bulk to admit $G$-symmetric topological boundary.
elements.sent-2411-113: As a remark, this theorem should be valid even for non-invertible symmetries as long as one considers strongly symmetric boundaries for RCFTs.
elements.sent-2411-114: Besides, the introduction of spin structure does not modify the construction, so theorem \ref{thm:G_sym_bdy automatically holds for fermionic CFTs.}
elements.sent-2411-116: In Ref.~\cite{Heinrich2018edge , a particular class of free boson CFTs with non-anomalous $\z_{2}$ symmetry is studied.}
elements.sent-2411-117: These CFTs can be gapped (hence its bulk TFT admit a gapped boundary) by adding local perturbations if we forget the $\z_{2}$-symmetry.
elements.sent-2411-118: However, it cannot be gapped if we restrict to $\z_{2}$-symmetric local perturbations.
elements.sent-2411-119: This suggests its bulk TQFT does not admit a $\z_{2}$-symmetric topological boundary condition.
elements.sent-2411-120: Hence by theorem \ref{thm:G_sym_bdy , this $\z_{2}$-symmetric CFTs do not admit conformal boundary conditions while preserving $\z_{2}$-symmetry.}
elements.sent-2411-121: We also note that if these CFTs admit a lattice regularization\footnote{Currently, we do not have an explicit construction for them.}
elements.sent-2411-122: But from a field-theoretic point of view, there is no obstruction since it can be gapped after forgetting $\z_{2}$, in particular chiral central charge and all higher central charges must vanish.
elements.sent-2411-123: If $\z_{2}$-action is on-site, by the same argument used in Ref.~\cite{Hellerman2021grav (reviewed in Sec.~\ref{sec:GALR ), the free boundary condition would simultaneously preserve conformal symmetry and $\z_{2}$ symmetry in the IR limit, a contradiction.}}
elements.sent-2411-124: Thus, in these models (if exist), this non-anomalous $\z_{2}$ symmetry cannot act in an on-site way.
elements.sent-2411-125: This suggests that there should more obstructions for a symmetry to be on-site beyond anomalies.
elements.sent-2411-126: In this paper, we identified higher obstructions for 2d RCFT to admit a conformal boundary condition or a lattice regularization.
elements.sent-2411-127: This disproves the widely believed conjecture that vanishing of gravitational anomaly should imply a conformal boundary condition \cite{Hellerman2021grav,Moller2024VOA .}
elements.sent-2411-128: However, these higher obstructions, known as higher central charges are now only defined for rational CFTs and local expressions for these quantities are still lacking.
elements.sent-2411-129: By a local expression, we mean that expressing higher central charges by certain correlation functions or OPEs.
elements.sent-2411-130: It will be interesting to find such expressions and extend the definition of higher central charges to irrational CFTs or even general 2d QFTs.
elements.sent-2411-131: We also discussed the case if the conformal boundary is required to preserve global internal symmetries.
elements.sent-2411-132: It turns out in certain examples \cite{Heinrich2018edge the $G$-symmetric conformal boundary conditions can be absent even if there is no $G$-anomaly or gravitational anomaly.}
elements.sent-2411-133: This suggests some subtle generalization of “mixed gravitational-$G$ anomaly” in these examples.
elements.sent-2411-134: We also point out the method to detect such “mixed G-gravitational anomaly” in Ref.~\cite{Heinrich2018edge is extremely complicated.}
elements.sent-2411-135: It is natural to ask if there is a $G$-enriched version of higher central charges\footnote{In the case of fermionic theories and $G=\U$, certain obstructions known as higher Hall conductivities are proposed in Ref.~\cite{Kobayashi2022boundary,Kobayashi2024higherhall .}}
elements.sent-2411-136: We will not discuss them here.
elements.sent-2411-137: Besides, it is shown in Ref.~\cite{Kaidi2022highercentralcharge that there is a Galois action on space of rational CFTs, under which a higher central charge of a CFT is exactly the chiral central charge of its Galois conjugate CFT.}
elements.sent-2411-138: Besides, these authors also showed that a topological boundary of 3d TQFT remains topological after Galois action.
elements.sent-2411-139: It is interestin to see if similar argument can be extended to the symmetry-enriched cases.
elements.sent-2411-140: For example, it is shown in Ref.~\cite{Buican2022Galois that under some mild conditions, the Galois action in 3d TQFT preserve all the global symmetries (\eg 0-form, 1-form or 2-group symmetries).}
elements.sent-2411-141: It is intriguing to extend this result to boundary CFTs.

elements.section-2411-007: A proof for conformal boundary and topological interface
elements.sent-2411-146: In this appendix, we provide a more rigorous proof to theorem \ref{thm:conf_BC used in the main text.}
elements.sent-2411-147: In mathematics, chiral halves of 2d conformal field theories are described by vertex operator algebras (VOAs).
elements.sent-2411-148: See e.g. Ref.~\cite{frenkel2004vertex,Fuchs23VOA for its definition.}
elements.sent-2411-149: We do not need this definition explicitly through out this appendix.
elements.sent-2411-150: Following that convention, below we will identify a chiral CFT with a vertex operator algebra $\V$, and we will denote $\bar{\V}$ for its orientation-reversal.
elements.sent-2411-151: First let us review the definition of conformal defects and topological defects, following Ref.~\cite{Fuchs2007defects .}
elements.sent-2411-152: For two full CFTs, the conformal defect between them is defined as \beq (T_{1 (z)-\bar{T _{1 (\bar{z ))|_{D =(T_{2 (z)-\bar{T _{2 (\bar{z ))|_{D \eeq where $D$ is the defect.}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-153: The physical meaning of conformal defect is that the component of energy-momentum tensor normal to the defect $D$ is continuous across $D$.
elements.sent-2411-154: In the case $T_{2 (z)=0=\bar{T _{2 (\bar{z )$ (\ie the second theory is empty), this definition reduces to conformal boundary of the first theory.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-155: In particular, if we have \beq \begin{split T_{1 (z)|_{D =T_{2 (z)|_{D \bar{T _{1 (z)|_{D =\bar{T _{2 (z)|_{D \end{split \eeq then the defect $D$ is said to be topological.}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-156: To gain some intuition, defects satisfying Eq.~\eqref{eq:topo_def commute with infinitesimal diffeomorphisms (generated by energy-momentum tensor!).}
elements.sent-2411-157: As a consequence, one can deform the topological defect $D$ inside the correlators without changing the value of correlators as long as $D$ does not cross any operator insertions.
elements.sent-2411-158: In particular, we note that if both theories are chiral, \ie $\bar{T _{1 =0=\bar{T _{2}$, then a conformal defect is automatically topological.}}}
elements.sent-2411-159: For the case of theorem \ref{thm:conf_BC , this means a conformal boundary condition of $\V_{1}\otimes\bar{\V _{2}$ is equivalent to a topological defect between $\V_{1}$ and $\V_{2}$ by folding trick.}}
elements.sent-2411-160: This can happen, for example $\V_{2}$ can be obtained by orbifolding $\V_{1}$ (known as half gauging in Ref.~\cite{shao2023whats ).}
elements.sent-2411-161: It is conjectured in Ref.~\cite{Moller2024VOA there is a topological defect separating $\V_{1}$ and $\V_{2}$ if and only if they are related by orbifold construction.}
elements.sent-2411-162: For example, in the case of Ising model (minimal model $\mathcal{M _{3,4}$), In this case $\V_{1}\simeq \V_{2}$ and This model contains 3 primaries, denoted by $1,\sigma,\epsilon$.}
elements.sent-2411-163: The topological defects correspond to global symmetry of the (chiral) theory and they are precisely Verlinde lines in this case (labeled by primaries).
elements.sent-2411-164: It is also known that in Ising model, its conformal boundary states are Cardy states, which are labeled by primaries as well.
elements.sent-2411-165: So they are in one-to-one correspondence, as is claimed.
elements.sent-2411-166: As another example, we can look at the 3-state Potts model \cite{Sinha2024Potts (minimal model $\mathcal{M _{5,6}$ in $D$-series \cite{DiFrancesco1997modular ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-167: This model has 8 conformal boundary states \cite{Runkel2000minimal while it has 8 (simple) topological defects \cite{Sinha2024Potts .}}
elements.sent-2411-168: A further example is boson at radius $R=\frac{n {m R_{s.d.}$ where $R_{s.d.}$ is the self-dual radius and $m,n$ are coprime positive integers.}}
elements.sent-2411-169: In this case, the moduli space of conformal boundary states is given by \cite{Gaberdiel2001boundary \beq \mathcal{M =S^{3 /(\z_{m}\times\z_{n ) \eeq This coincides with the topological defects obtained in Ref.~\cite{Fuchs2007boson .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-170: In the language of vertex operator algebras (VOA), primary fields appear as (twisted) modules of VOAs.
elements.sent-2411-171: Given a topological defect $D$ between two strongly rational VOAs $\V_{1}$ and $\V_{2}$.
elements.sent-2411-172: This gives rise to a wrapping operation, denoted by $F_{D}$.
elements.sent-2411-173: Note that there is no divergences during shrinking since $D$ is a topological defect.
elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[width=0.5\linewidth]{Shrinking.png} \caption{Wrapping the local primary field $\phi(z)$ in $\V_{1}$ by a topological defect $D$. Shrinking the defect gives rise to a new local field $F_{D}(\phi)$ in $\V_{2}$, which is generally not simple in $\Rep(\V_{2})$.} \label{fig:shrinking}
elements.figure-2411-005: \includegraphics[width=0.7\linewidth]{fusion.png} \caption{The functor $F_{D}$ is (lax) monoidal, in the sense that the last arrow above does not need to be isomorphism.} \label{fig:enter-label}
elements.sent-2411-174: We also need to include twisted modules (a.k.a twisted sectors).
elements.sent-2411-175: Twisted modules are created by primary operators attached to a topological line defect (see Fig.~\ref{fig:lasso , where primary $\sigma$ is attached to line defect $D_{3}$).}
elements.sent-2411-176: The wrapping construction is generalized to so-called lasso operation in Ref.~\cite{Chang2019defect for twisted sectors, see Fig.~\ref{fig:lasso .}}
elements.sent-2411-177: Importantly, one has to fix the choice of the junction $v\in \mathrm{Hom (D_{3}\otimes D_{2 ,D_{1}\otimes D_{2 )$ where $\otimes$ means fusion of defects\footnote{In the untwisted case, there is only one canonical choice for the junction such that $v=\mathrm{id _{D}\in\mathrm{Hom (D,D)$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-178: This is also what we have assumed so far.. .
elements.figure-2411-006: \includegraphics[width=0.4\linewidth]{Lasso.png} \caption{The action of topological defect $D_{2}$ on the twisted sector $\cH_{D_{3}}$. The action may bring the states to another twisted sector $\cH_{D_{1}}$.} \label{fig:lasso}
elements.sent-2411-179: One can see the lasso operation is also monoidal by a similar argument used before.
elements.sent-2411-181: Given two strongly rational VOA $\V_{1 ,\V_{2}$ and a topological defect $D$ between them, the functor $F_{D :\Rep(\V_{1 )\to\Rep(\V_{2 )$ constructed above is (lax) monoidal.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-182: Mathematically, it is well known that a monoidal functor can be viewed as a bimodule.
elements.sent-2411-183: Let us write $\cC_{i =\Rep(\V_{i ),i=1,2$, then $F_{D}$ becomes a $(\cC_{1 ,\cC_{2 )$-bimodule.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-184: Recall that in Ref.~\cite{Huang05VOA it is shown that $\Rep(\V_{i ),i=1,2$ are modular tensor categories, which give the bulk TQFT by Reshetikhin-Turaev construction.}}
elements.sent-2411-185: The physical meaning of $F_{D}$ as a $(\cC_{1 ,\cC_{2 )$-bimodule is that it labels a topological interface between these two bulk TQFTs \cite{Fuchs13bicat .}}}
elements.sent-2411-186: Note that this bimodule (equivalently interface) depends only on the topological defect $D$ (or the conformal boundary condition in the main text).
elements.sent-2411-187: Given the defect $D$ it is uniquely determined.
